content.problemStatement:
**Q2) OR B):** You are working on an inventory management system. The system provides a list of items recorded in a single line. This list is a mix of item quantities (integers) and item descriptions (strings). Your task is to write a Python program that processes this list and calculates the total sum of all item quantities.

You must implement a function that iterates through the given list. This function should add up all the integer values while gracefully ignoring the string descriptions. You are required to use a `try-except` block to handle the type mismatch when an element is not a valid integer.


Input Format
A single line of text containing space-separated values. These values can be integers or strings.



Output Format
A single integer representing the sum of all the integer values found in the input list.
constraints: Each test case line contains at most 15 tokens.
Integer values fit in the 32‑bit signed range.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T (1 ≤ T ≤ 10), the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a space‑separated list of tokens. A token is either a valid integer (may be negative) or a non‑numeric string.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the sum of all integer tokens in that test case.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: integers 10, 20, 30 sum to 60. Test case 2: -5 + 15 = 10. Test case 3: no integers, sum = 0.
All three tokens are integers, sum = 100 + 200 + 300 = 600.
First line has no integers → 0. Second line sums -1 -2 -3 = -6.

solution.approach: For each test case read the whole line as a string and split it by whitespace to obtain tokens. Iterate over the tokens and try to convert each token to an integer. In languages that support exceptions (Python, C++, Java) wrap the conversion in a try‑catch block; if conversion succeeds, add the value to a running total, otherwise ignore the token. After processing all tokens output the total. The algorithm is linear in the number of tokens.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * L) where L is the number of tokens in a test case (≤ 15).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) additional space besides the input line.
